description: Retrieves rows from one or more tables or views. The fundamental read operation in SQL.
example: SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE active = true;

description: Specifies the table, view, subquery, or join source from which to retrieve data.
example: SELECT * FROM orders o JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id;

description: Filters rows based on a condition. Applied before grouping and aggregation.
example: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > 100 AND in_stock = true;

description: Combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column. INNER JOIN is the default when JOIN is used without a qualifier.
syntax: SELECT ... FROM table1 JOIN table2 ON table1.col = table2.col
example: SELECT u.name, o.total FROM users u JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

description: Returns all rows from the left table and matched rows from the right table. Unmatched right-table columns are NULL.
syntax: SELECT ... FROM table1 LEFT JOIN table2 ON table1.col = table2.col
example: SELECT u.name, o.id FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

description: Returns all rows from the right table and matched rows from the left table. Unmatched left-table columns are NULL.
syntax: SELECT ... FROM table1 RIGHT JOIN table2 ON table1.col = table2.col
example: SELECT u.name, o.id FROM users u RIGHT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

description: Returns all rows from both tables. Where there is no match, NULLs fill in the missing side.
syntax: SELECT ... FROM table1 FULL OUTER JOIN table2 ON table1.col = table2.col
example: SELECT a.id, b.id FROM table_a a FULL OUTER JOIN table_b b ON a.key = b.key;

description: Returns only rows where the join condition is met in both tables. Equivalent to plain JOIN.
syntax: SELECT ... FROM table1 INNER JOIN table2 ON table1.col = table2.col
example: SELECT p.name, c.name FROM products p INNER JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.id;

description: Produces a Cartesian product of two tables, returning every combination of rows from both tables.
syntax: SELECT ... FROM table1 CROSS JOIN table2
example: SELECT colors.name, sizes.label FROM colors CROSS JOIN sizes;

description: Groups rows sharing the same values in specified columns so aggregate functions can be applied per group.
syntax: SELECT col, AGG(col2) FROM table GROUP BY col
example: SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS headcount FROM employees GROUP BY department;

description: Filters groups produced by GROUP BY based on aggregate conditions. Applied after grouping, unlike WHERE.
syntax: SELECT col, AGG(col2) FROM table GROUP BY col HAVING condition
example: SELECT department, AVG(salary) FROM employees GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(salary) > 70000;

description: Sorts the result set by one or more columns in ascending (ASC, default) or descending (DESC) order.
example: SELECT name, created_at FROM posts ORDER BY created_at DESC;

description: Restricts the number of rows returned by a query. Widely supported; equivalent to TOP in SQL Server or FETCH FIRST in ANSI SQL.
example: SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY popularity DESC LIMIT 10;

description: Skips a specified number of rows before returning results. Commonly used with LIMIT for pagination.
example: SELECT * FROM articles ORDER BY published_at DESC LIMIT 20 OFFSET 40;

description: Combines result sets of two SELECT statements, removing duplicate rows. Both queries must have the same number and compatible column types.
example: SELECT email FROM customers UNION SELECT email FROM newsletter_subscribers;

description: Combines result sets of two SELECT statements including all duplicates. Faster than UNION as no deduplication is performed.
example: SELECT id FROM table_a UNION ALL SELECT id FROM table_b;

description: Returns only rows that appear in both SELECT result sets.
example: SELECT user_id FROM premium_users INTERSECT SELECT user_id FROM active_users;

description: Returns rows from the first SELECT result set that are not present in the second. Known as MINUS in Oracle.
example: SELECT user_id FROM all_users EXCEPT SELECT user_id FROM banned_users;

description: Eliminates duplicate rows from the result set. Applied after SELECT to return only unique combinations of selected columns.
example: SELECT DISTINCT country FROM customers ORDER BY country;

description: Creates an alias for a column or table name within a query. Aliases improve readability and are required when referencing calculated columns.
example: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name FROM employees;

description: Defines one or more Common Table Expressions (CTEs) that can be referenced in the main query. Improves readability and enables recursive queries.
syntax: WITH cte_name AS (SELECT ...) SELECT ... FROM cte_name
example: WITH recent_orders AS (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days') SELECT COUNT(*) FROM recent_orders;

description: Tests for the existence of any row in a subquery. Returns TRUE if the subquery returns at least one row.
syntax: SELECT ... WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ... WHERE condition)
example: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.id);

description: Adds new rows to a table. Can insert a single row with VALUES or multiple rows from a SELECT subquery.
syntax: INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2)
example: INSERT INTO users (name, email, created_at) VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@example.com', NOW());

description: Specifies the literal data rows to insert in an INSERT statement, or constructs a virtual table of values.
syntax: INSERT INTO table (cols) VALUES (val1, val2), (val3, val4)
example: INSERT INTO colors (name, hex) VALUES ('red', '#FF0000'), ('blue', '#0000FF');

description: Modifies existing rows in a table. Always use a WHERE clause to avoid updating all rows unintentionally.
syntax: UPDATE table SET col = value WHERE condition
example: UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.1 WHERE category = 'electronics';

description: Specifies the column-value assignments in an UPDATE statement.
syntax: UPDATE table SET col1 = val1, col2 = val2 WHERE condition
example: UPDATE users SET last_login = NOW(), login_count = login_count + 1 WHERE id = 42;

description: Removes rows from a table that match the WHERE condition. Without WHERE, deletes all rows (use TRUNCATE for full-table clears).
example: DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expires_at < NOW();

description: Performs INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE on a target table based on matching rows in a source. Also known as UPSERT in some dialects.
syntax: MERGE INTO target USING source ON condition WHEN MATCHED THEN ... WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN ...
example: MERGE INTO inventory AS t USING updates AS s ON t.product_id = s.product_id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.qty = s.qty WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (product_id, qty) VALUES (s.product_id, s.qty);

description: Removes all rows from a table quickly by deallocating data pages rather than deleting row-by-row. Cannot be rolled back in most databases.
example: TRUNCATE TABLE session_logs;

description: Defines a new table with its columns, data types, constraints, and other properties.
example: CREATE TABLE users (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW());

description: Creates an index on one or more table columns to improve query performance for lookups, sorts, and joins.
example: CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email);

description: Creates a named virtual table based on a SELECT query. Views can simplify complex queries and enforce access control.
example: CREATE VIEW active_users AS SELECT * FROM users WHERE deactivated_at IS NULL;

description: Modifies an existing table's structure: adding/dropping columns, changing data types, adding/removing constraints.
example: ALTER TABLE products ADD COLUMN discount_pct NUMERIC(5,2) DEFAULT 0;

description: Permanently removes a table and all its data from the database. This operation is irreversible without a backup.
example: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temp_import_data;

description: Removes an index from a table. Useful when an index is no longer needed or causes write overhead.
example: DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_old_search;

description: Grants database privileges (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, etc.) on objects to users or roles.
syntax: GRANT privilege ON object TO user_or_role
example: GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON orders TO app_user;

description: Removes previously granted privileges from users or roles.
syntax: REVOKE privilege ON object FROM user_or_role
example: REVOKE DELETE ON customers FROM reporting_role;

description: Starts an explicit database transaction. Changes made after BEGIN are not visible to other sessions until committed.
example: BEGIN; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2; COMMIT;

description: Saves all changes made during the current transaction, making them permanent and visible to other sessions.
example: BEGIN; DELETE FROM temp_data; COMMIT;

description: Undoes all changes made during the current transaction, restoring the database to the state before BEGIN.
example: BEGIN; UPDATE inventory SET qty = 0; ROLLBACK;

description: Creates a named point within a transaction to which a partial ROLLBACK can be performed without aborting the entire transaction.
example: BEGIN; INSERT INTO log (msg) VALUES ('start'); SAVEPOINT after_log; INSERT INTO orders (item) VALUES ('widget'); ROLLBACK TO after_log; COMMIT;

description: Tests whether a value matches any value in a list or subquery. Equivalent to multiple OR conditions.
syntax: column IN (value1, value2, ...) or column IN (SELECT ...)
example: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status IN ('pending', 'processing', 'shipped');

description: Tests whether a value falls within a range, inclusive of both endpoints.
example: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 10.00 AND 50.00;

description: Pattern-matches a string using wildcards: % for zero or more characters, _ for exactly one character. Case-sensitive in most engines.
example: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';

description: Case-insensitive pattern matching, equivalent to LIKE but ignoring case. Supported in PostgreSQL; use LOWER(col) LIKE in other databases.
example: SELECT * FROM products WHERE name ILIKE '%laptop%';

description: Tests whether a column value is NULL. Cannot use = NULL because NULL comparisons always return UNKNOWN.
example: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE phone IS NULL;

description: Tests whether a column value is not NULL. Returns TRUE for any non-NULL value.
example: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email_verified_at IS NOT NULL;

description: Negates a boolean condition. Can be used with IN, LIKE, BETWEEN, EXISTS, and IS NULL.
example: SELECT * FROM products WHERE NOT category IN ('archived', 'discontinued');

description: Logical conjunction — returns TRUE only when both operands are TRUE. Evaluated after NOT but before OR in operator precedence.
example: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department = 'Engineering' AND salary > 90000;

description: Logical disjunction — returns TRUE when at least one operand is TRUE. Lower precedence than AND; use parentheses to control evaluation.
example: SELECT * FROM support_tickets WHERE status = 'open' OR priority = 'critical';

description: Conditional expression returning different values based on evaluated conditions. Acts like an if-else within a query.
syntax: CASE WHEN condition THEN result [WHEN ...] [ELSE default] END
example: SELECT name, CASE WHEN score >= 90 THEN 'A' WHEN score >= 80 THEN 'B' ELSE 'C' END AS grade FROM students;

description: Returns the first non-NULL value from a list of arguments. Useful for providing default values when a column may be NULL.
example: SELECT COALESCE(nickname, first_name, 'Anonymous') AS display_name FROM users;

description: Returns NULL if two expressions are equal; otherwise returns the first expression. Useful to avoid division-by-zero errors.
example: SELECT total / NULLIF(quantity, 0) AS unit_price FROM line_items;

description: Converts a value from one data type to another explicitly. Standard ANSI syntax; dialects also support :: (PostgreSQL) and CONVERT().
example: SELECT CAST(price AS INTEGER) FROM products;

description: Defines the window frame for a window function. Specifies PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, and frame boundaries.
syntax: function() OVER ([PARTITION BY col] [ORDER BY col] [frame])
example: SELECT name, salary, AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_avg FROM employees;

description: Divides the result set into partitions for window functions to operate on independently, similar to GROUP BY but without collapsing rows.
example: SELECT name, dept, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank FROM employees;

description: Assigns a unique sequential integer to each row within its partition, starting at 1. Ties receive different numbers.
syntax: ROW_NUMBER() OVER ([PARTITION BY col] ORDER BY col)
example: SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY created_at) AS order_num FROM orders;

description: Assigns a rank to each row within its partition. Ties receive the same rank, and gaps are left in the sequence.
syntax: RANK() OVER ([PARTITION BY col] ORDER BY col)
example: SELECT name, score, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS rank FROM leaderboard;

description: Like RANK but without gaps in ranking values after ties. Sequential integers are assigned with equal ranks for ties.
syntax: DENSE_RANK() OVER ([PARTITION BY col] ORDER BY col)
example: SELECT name, score, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS dense_rank FROM leaderboard;

description: Returns the value of a column from a previous row within the partition, without using a self-join. Useful for comparing a row to its predecessor.
syntax: LAG(column, offset, default) OVER ([PARTITION BY col] ORDER BY col)
example: SELECT date, revenue, LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS prev_revenue FROM daily_sales;

description: Returns the value of a column from a following row within the partition. The complement of LAG.
syntax: LEAD(column, offset, default) OVER ([PARTITION BY col] ORDER BY col)
example: SELECT date, revenue, LEAD(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS next_revenue FROM daily_sales;

description: Returns the first value in an ordered window frame. Useful for retrieving the earliest or lowest value per partition.
syntax: FIRST_VALUE(column) OVER ([PARTITION BY col] ORDER BY col [frame])
example: SELECT name, salary, FIRST_VALUE(name) OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS top_earner FROM employees;

description: Returns the last value in an ordered window frame. Requires explicit frame specification (ROWS BETWEEN ... AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING) to work as expected.
syntax: LAST_VALUE(column) OVER ([PARTITION BY col] ORDER BY col ROWS BETWEEN CURRENT ROW AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING)
example: SELECT name, salary, LAST_VALUE(name) OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING) AS lowest_earner FROM employees;
